text: The surgeon marks the supraumbilical port site using an Induco Karamine Sketch Pen, which is anti-infectant. The camera is positioned through this port, while the right hand port is placed 7.5 centimeters lateral and below, and the left hand port is established.

A contralateral port is established as a mirror image of the left-hand port. A small stab wound is made supraumbilically, and a Veress needle is introduced. The surgeon lifts the abdominal wall to ensure the needle is perpendicular, and upon hearing two clicks, enters the abdomen. The irrigation and suction tests are performed, followed by the hanging drop test. The abdominal wall is lifted, and the drop is sucked, indicating successful entry. Pneumoperitoneum is initiated, requiring approximately three liters of gas to reach the preset pressure of fifteen mmHg, with an initial flow rate of one liter per minute.

After creating pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon removes the Veress needle and enlarges the incision to 11 mm. Using a double-hand technique, a trocar is introduced, followed by the telescope for initial diagnostic laparoscopy. The laparoscopic view shows the posterior wall deep intramural myoma. Vasopressin diluted in saline is injected into the myoma, causing transient ischemia and hydrodissection, resulting in a marble white appearance of the uterus. A myoma screw is applied to the myoma.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to make an elliptical incision on the deep intramural myoma. An assistant helps by placing a myoma screw on the myoma for stabilization. The incision is enlarged from the opposite side, and a second myoma screw is used in the left hand to aid in enucleation. The deep muscle is carefully cut, and suction irrigation is employed to separate the tissue, while the harmonic scalpel is used cautiously to avoid breakage.

The surgeon uses suction irrigation to enucleate a six-centimeter myoma from the uterine cavity, revealing the open cavity. The myoma, previously occupying fifty percent of the uterine lumen, is fully separated. Additional myomas are identified and removed using a harmonic scalpel, including a left cornual myoma, which is also successfully enucleated.

The surgeon begins suturing the uterine wall, applying extracorporeal Western knots to close the filling defect and approximate the tissue layers. The knots are pulled tight, visibly opposing the wall without any bleeding. The surgeon continues with intracorporeal suturing, using a Dandy jamming knot and continuous suturing technique to approximate the deep muscular layer.

The surgeon uses the left hand to hold the suture while the right hand takes bites with a number one white suture and round body needle, interlocking the suture to approximate the margins of the incision. The suturing is performed quickly to prevent bleeding, aided by the vasopressin effect.

The surgeon performs deep muscular continuous suturing, approximating the margins of the incision. The assistant holds the tail of an extracorporeal western knot, while the surgeon interlocks the sutures to secure the closure. A flap of muscle is pushed inside, and the suture is placed over it to close a small tear. The suturing technique involves tight interlocking to ensure strength, with a double layer of sutures applied for reinforcement.

The surgeon completes the suturing of the uterine layers using various knots, including Skinner surgeon's knot for the open cavity, tumble square knot for the muscular layer, and extracorporeal square or misrash knot for tension opposition. The Dundee jamming knot is used for continuous suturing, and Everdeen termination is applied for the serous layer. The suturing is tightly closed, and suction irrigation is performed to ensure minimal blood loss. A 15mm morcellator is used to morcellate the tissue, followed by port closure using a cobbler's needle to push and pull the suture through the port site.
